stories.title: The Two-Thirty Crowd
stories.chapter1_html:
The Blue Hour Diner did its real business after midnight, when the rest of Eddison Street had gone dark and the only light left on the block leaked out through its fogged front windows in a long yellow rectangle. Nessa had worked the overnight shift for six years, which meant she knew the two-thirty crowd the way other people knew their own families. Better, maybe. You learned a lot about a person when they were tired enough to stop pretending.

By two-fifteen the booths had filled in their usual order. Marcus, the night nurse from St. Adelaide's, folded into the corner with his scrubs still smelling faintly of disinfectant, ordering the same thing he always ordered and never finishing it. Two cab drivers near the window, arguing in low friendly voices about a bridge that had been closed for repairs since spring. Old Teodora with her crossword, a pencil tucked behind one ear and a second one already lost somewhere in her enormous handbag. And in the booth by the pie case, where the heater rattled loudest, the regulars simply called him the Professor, though nobody actually knew if he taught anything at all.

'Coffee, Ness,' Marcus said, not looking up. 'And tell me something good.'

'Define good,' she said, already pouring.

'Not a code. Not a fire. Not a man who swallowed a fork on a dare.' He rubbed his eyes. 'Somebody actually do that?'

'Tuesday,' she said. 'He was fine. The fork was fine. Everybody lived.' She set the cup down and he wrapped both hands around it like it was the only warm thing in the world, which at three in the morning in February it very nearly was.

This was the thing nobody told you about the overnight shift: it was lonely, and it was also the least lonely place Nessa had ever been. The daytime people came in and out like weather, indifferent, in a hurry. But the night people came back. The same faces, the same orders, the same small confessions slipped across the counter between refills. Teodora's son who never called. The younger cabbie's daughter starting school in the fall. Marcus and the patients he couldn't save and couldn't stop carrying home in his chest. They had become, without anyone deciding it, a kind of household. A family assembled out of the people who happened to be awake when everyone else was asleep.

Nessa had not planned to belong to anyone, least of all this. She'd taken the overnight shift because it paid a little more and because it meant she didn't have to lie awake in her apartment listening to the building settle and think about all the ways her life had not gone the way she'd imagined at twenty. The night shift was supposed to be a holding pattern, a thing she did until she figured out what she was actually doing. Six years on, she had stopped pretending it was temporary. The Blue Hour was where she lived. The plastic-coated menus she could recite in her sleep, the burn scar on her wrist from the first month, the particular squeal the freezer door made — these were the furniture of her real life, and the people who sat in the booths were the only ones who'd ever seen all of her: tired, unguarded, generous in the specific way you can only be at four in the morning when nothing you say will be remembered in daylight.

And of all of them, the Professor was the one she would have said she knew the least and trusted the most. He never told her anything about himself. He never asked her anything she didn't want to answer. He simply arrived, every night, and let her pour his tea, and listened — really listened, in a way almost nobody did anymore — when she talked, which she found herself doing more than she ever did with anyone, precisely because he asked for nothing back. He was the still point the whole long night turned around. He was, she sometimes thought without quite letting herself finish the thought, the closest thing to a father she'd had since her own had stopped speaking to her years ago, for reasons that had seemed enormous at the time and now only seemed sad.

Which was why, when the Professor didn't come in, Nessa noticed.

She noticed at two-forty, when his booth stayed empty. She noticed harder at three, when she found herself wiping down a table that was already clean just so she could keep an eye on the door. The Professor had come in every single night for as long as she had worked there. Snowstorms. The night the power went out and they ran the place on candles and a camp stove. The night his hands shook so badly he couldn't hold the menu and she had read the specials to him out loud, and neither of them ever mentioned it again. He came in at two-thirty, he ordered tea and one slice of whatever pie was oldest because he said it needed the company, and he left a tip that was always, exactly, too much.

'He's just late,' said Marcus, when she mentioned it. But he said it the way you say a thing you want to be true.

By three-thirty the cabbies had gone. By four Teodora had filled in most of her crossword and left, patting Nessa's hand on the way out the way she always did, telling her to eat something, you're too thin, which Nessa was not. The diner emptied toward the gray edge of morning until it was only her and Marcus and the hum of the pie case and the empty booth by the heater, which had never once, in six years, been empty at this hour.

'I'm going to call,' Nessa said.

'You have his number?'

She didn't, and that was its own small shock, the realization that she had known this man for six years and could not have told you his last name. None of them could. He was the Professor. He was the tea and the oldest pie and the too-large tip and the quiet that sat with him in the booth like a companion. He was family in the way the night made family, which was to say completely, and not at all.

'No,' she admitted. 'I don't.'

Marcus was quiet for a moment. Then he reached into the pocket of his scrubs and took out a folded square of paper, soft and worn at the creases like it had been opened and closed many times.

'He gave me this,' Marcus said. 'Last night. Before he left. I thought it was weird but I didn't, I don't know. I figured I'd ask him about it tonight.' He turned it over in his hands. 'He said, give this to Nessa if I forget to come back. I thought he meant, like, if he fell asleep. If he overslept. I thought it was a joke.'

'If he forgot to come back,' Nessa repeated.

The diner felt suddenly very large and very quiet around them. Outside, the first cold blue of dawn was coming up over Eddison Street, and the long yellow rectangle of the window light was fading into it, the way it did every morning, the way it had every morning she had ever worked here.

Marcus held out the paper. Her name was on the front in careful, old-fashioned handwriting, the kind they didn't teach anymore. Just her name. Nessa.

She wiped her hands on her apron though they were dry. She took it. The paper was light as nothing and somehow heavier than anything she'd held all night, and she stood there in the empty diner with the empty booth behind her and the dawn coming up, not yet ready to unfold it, knowing that whatever was written inside, the two-thirty crowd would never be exactly the same again.

She slid her thumb under the fold.
stories.chapter2_html:
Inside, the handwriting was smaller, crowded, as though he'd had a great deal to say and very little room to say it.

Nessa, it began. If you are reading this, then I have done the thing I have been quietly planning for some time, and I am sorry not to have told you in person. You would have tried to talk me out of it, and you would have been kind about it, and that would have been worse.

She lowered the paper. Marcus was watching her face. 'Well?' he said. 'Is he, is it bad?'

She read the next line and felt something unknot in her chest, then re-knot somewhere lower and stranger.

There is a key taped under my booth, the note went on. The one by the heater. It opens a storage unit on Fenwick Road, number 114. Everything in it is yours now — all of you. I should have given it to you years ago. Go in the daylight. Bring the others. And please, for once, let someone leave you too much.

Nessa was already crouching, reaching under the booth by the heater, her fingers searching the cold underside of the table until they found it: a small brass key, taped exactly where he'd said, warm now from the rattling heat, waiting. She pulled it free and held it up to the gray morning light, and Marcus said, very softly, 'What's on Fenwick Road?'

'I don't know,' Nessa said. 'But he wanted us to find out together.'

comprehension.question: What does Marcus do for a living?

stories.title: The Wrong Umbrella
stories.chapter1_html:
It was not Della's umbrella, and that was the first thing.

She discovered this on the bus, three stops from home, when the rain had finally let up enough to let her examine the thing she had grabbed in her hurry out of the lost-and-found bin at the library where she worked. Her own umbrella was a sensible navy blue, bought at the chemist's for four pounds, missing one of its little plastic end-caps. This umbrella was black, yes, and roughly the same size, but the handle was carved wood, worn smooth and pale at the curve where a hand would rest, and tucked into the inside of the canopy, where the ribs met the shaft, someone had tied a small brass tag on a length of red thread.

The tag was engraved. Return to 14 Sorrel Lane. There is no reward. There is only the right thing.

Della read it three times. Then she missed her stop.

She was not, ordinarily, a woman who went looking for adventures. She catalogued returned books and shelved them in the correct order and found a deep, unfashionable satisfaction in a thing being where it belonged. But there was something about the tag — the strange confidence of it, the slightly scolding tone, the way it assumed she would do the right thing simply because it had told her to — that lodged in her like a splinter. She got off at the next stop and looked up Sorrel Lane on her phone and found that it was, improbably, only a ten-minute walk away, in a part of the neighborhood she'd somehow never had reason to visit.

Sorrel Lane turned out to be a short crooked street of narrow brick houses, the kind that leaned companionably toward one another over their tiny front gardens. Number 14 was at the very end. It had a green door with a brass knocker shaped like a fox, and window boxes full of something that had gone to seed, and an air of having been waiting for her specifically, which Della knew was nonsense, and could not entirely shake.

She knocked.

Nothing. She knocked again, and was halfway through composing a small apologetic speech in her head — so sorry to bother you, I believe this is your umbrella — when she noticed the second thing.

There was a note pinned beneath the fox knocker. Not for her; it was weathered, the paper soft and grayed, as though it had been there for weeks. But the handwriting on it was the same careful hand as the brass tag. Mrs. Ackroyd, it read, if you have come about the umbrella, please do not leave it on the step. Knock at number 11 instead. — H.W.

Della was not Mrs. Ackroyd. But she had, undeniably, come about the umbrella.

She looked down the lane to number 11. It had a red door, and a cat sitting in the window watching her with the frank, unhurried judgment that cats reserve for people who are about to do something foolish.

This, Della thought, was the point at which a sensible woman went home. She had tried. She had knocked. She had done a reasonable amount of the right thing, and nobody could fault her for leaving the umbrella tucked behind the window box and getting on with her evening. She had a frozen dinner and a library book of her own waiting. The rain was starting up again.

She went to number 11.

The door opened before she reached it, which startled her badly, and a small elderly man peered out at her with bright, birdlike eyes behind enormous spectacles. He looked at her face, and then he looked down at the umbrella in her hand, and his whole expression changed — not to relief, exactly, but to something more complicated. Something almost like dread.

'Oh,' he said. 'Oh dear. It's come back, then.'

'I'm sorry?' said Della. 'I found this at the library, the lost-and-found, and the tag said—'

'I know what the tag says.' He did not take the umbrella. He looked at it the way you might look at a letter you'd been avoiding opening. 'I wrote the tag. A long time ago. I rather hoped—' He stopped. 'You'd better come in. You can't stand out there in the rain holding that thing, it isn't right.'

'I really should just—'

'It belonged to my brother,' the man said. 'The umbrella. He left it behind the last time I saw him, which was eleven years ago this March, and he has not been seen since.' He said this plainly, the way you'd state the weather. 'I put that tag on it and gave it to a great many people over the years. Asked them to pass it along. Lost-and-founds, mostly. Charity shops. I suppose I thought — I don't know what I thought. That it might find its way to wherever he'd got to. That he might recognize it. Foolishness.' His bright eyes fixed on her. 'And now here it is back again. After all this time. Which means it has been a great many places, and met a great many hands, and you, miss, are the first person in eleven years to actually bring it to the door.'

Della stood on the step with the rain coming down and the cat watching and the dead window boxes nodding in the wind, and she did not know what to say.

'There's something written inside it,' the man went on. 'Did you find it? Not the tag. Inside the handle.'

'Inside the handle?' Della turned the umbrella over. The carved wooden handle was solid, smooth, unremarkable. 'No, I — there's nothing—'

'There's a seam,' he said quietly. 'You have to know where to press. May I?'

She handed it to him. His thin fingers found a spot near the curve of the handle, pressed, and twisted, and with a soft click the bottom of the handle came away in his palm — hollow, she saw, the wood drilled out into a little chamber — and inside, rolled tight and yellowed with age, was a slip of paper that had clearly been waiting there a very long time.

The man went quite still.

'That's not mine,' he whispered. 'I never put anything in there. I didn't know it opened.'

He unrolled it with trembling hands. Della leaned in despite herself. There was a single line written on the paper, in a hand that was not the careful hand of the tag, a hurried slanting scrawl, and as the man read it his face drained of all its color.

'What does it say?' Della asked. 'What is it?'

He looked up at her, and the rain ran down the green door of the house his brother had told people to knock at, and he said, in a voice gone suddenly very small: 'It says he knew. It says he knew this would come back to me. And it tells me where to find him.'
stories.chapter2_html:
'Where?' Della said. 'Where does it say?'

But the man had sat down heavily on his own doorstep, the slip of paper shaking in his hand, and for a moment she thought he wasn't going to answer at all.

'You don't understand,' he said. 'My brother didn't disappear by accident. The police said he must have, that he wandered off, that his mind was going. I let them say it. It was easier than the truth.' He smoothed the paper against his knee. 'The truth is he left on purpose. He left because of something he'd found out — about our family, about the house, about the man we'd both spent our whole lives believing was our father. And he hid this where only I would ever look, because he knew that one day, when the umbrella came home, it would mean I was finally ready to look.'

Della crouched down on the wet step beside him, library book and frozen dinner entirely forgotten. 'Ready to look for what?'

The man turned the paper toward her at last. Two words, and a number. An address she half-recognized — a village out past the reservoir, a place people only drove through.

'For whatever he found,' the man said. 'It's still there. He's telling me it's still there. After eleven years.' He looked at her with his bright, frightened eyes. 'I can't go alone. I'm too old and I'm too afraid. But you brought it back, miss. That has to mean something.' He swallowed. 'Will you come with me?'

stories.title: Low Tide at Halverston
stories.chapter1_html:
The off-season was the only time Joel liked Halverston, which was probably why he'd timed the trip for November, when the boardwalk shops put up their plywood shutters and the wind came off the gray water hard enough to make your eyes sting. The summer Halverston — the one with the fudge and the fortune-teller and the carousel that played the same six bars of music until you wanted to scream — that Halverston belonged to other people. This one, the cold quiet one, had always felt like his.

His and Marin's. But he was trying not to think about that.

He'd come back to close up his grandmother's cottage, which the family had finally agreed to sell, and he'd told himself it would take a weekend and that he would feel nothing in particular. He was thirty-four years old. He had not lived in this town in twelve years. The girl he'd spent every summer of his adolescence with had become a series of increasingly infrequent updates from other people — Marin's gone to art school, Marin's living abroad, Marin's back, did you hear — until even those had stopped, and she'd become simply a thing that had happened to him once, a long time ago, when he was young enough to believe a summer could last.

So he was not prepared, walking out onto the long mudflats at low tide with a cup of gas-station coffee going cold in his hand, to see someone already out there. A single figure, far out where the water had pulled back to reveal the ribbed gray sand, crouched and intent over something on the ground. He almost turned back. He had come here to be alone. But there was something in the set of the shoulders, the particular way the figure tilted its head, that stopped him where he stood with the wind pulling at his coat.

He would have known her anywhere. That was the terrible thing. Twelve years and he would have known her from the back, from a quarter mile, from the way she crouched.

He could have left. The flats were enormous; she hadn't seen him; he could have walked back up to the cottage and finished wrapping his grandmother's chipped blue plates in newspaper and driven home on Sunday and let Marin go on being a thing that had happened to him once. That was, he understood even as his feet started moving, almost certainly the sensible thing to do.

He walked out across the mud toward her instead.

She heard him when he was still thirty feet off and turned, and he watched the recognition move across her face in stages — a stranger, a familiar stranger, and then, all at once, him — and she stood up so fast she nearly lost her balance on the slick sand.

'Joel,' she said. Just that. His name, in her voice, after twelve years, with the wind trying to take it away.

'Hi, Marin.'

For a moment neither of them said anything. The gulls wheeled and complained overhead. The water lay far out at the edge of everything, a thin gray line, holding its breath the way it did at the very bottom of the tide before it turned and started coming back.

'You look exactly the same,' she said, which wasn't true, and they both knew it, and somehow it was the kindest thing she could have said.

'You're collecting sea glass,' he said, because it was the only thing he could think of, and because she was — he could see the little canvas bag at her feet, the same kind of bag, he thought, with a lurch, that she'd carried when they were seventeen, filling it with frosted green and brown and the rare, prized blue.

'I never stopped,' she said. She looked down at the bag, then back at him. 'I tell myself it's for a project. I make things now, did you, you probably didn't hear. Windows, mostly. Glass. I melt it down and—' She stopped. 'I'm babbling. I haven't seen you in twelve years and I'm talking about my job.'

'I like that you make windows,' he said, and meant it so much it frightened him.

They walked. It wasn't decided; it just happened, the way things had always just happened with Marin, the two of them falling into step across the wet gray flats with the town a low huddle of shuttered shops behind them. She showed him a piece of glass she'd found, a fat teardrop of cobalt blue, rare as anything, and dropped it into his palm without asking and his fingers closed around it before he could think better of it.

'You kept the cottage,' she said. 'Your grandmother's.'

'We're selling it. That's why I'm here. This weekend.' He saw something flicker and close in her face and added, too quickly, 'It's just a weekend. I drive back Sunday.'

'Of course,' she said. 'Of course you do.'

And there it was, the old ache, the one he'd packed away so carefully twelve years ago that he'd half convinced himself it had never existed: the sense of standing next to the one person in the world he fit beside, on a clock that was already running out. They had always been a low-tide thing, he and Marin. A thing that existed only in the brief strange window when the water pulled back and revealed the ground beneath, knowing all the while that the tide would turn and cover it again. It had been summers, before. Now it was a single gray weekend in November.

'Can I ask you something,' she said, stopping. The wind had loosened her hair and she pushed it back with the back of her wrist, the way she always had. 'And you don't have to answer.'

'Okay.'

'Why did you never write back?' she said. 'That last summer. After you left. I wrote you, Joel. I wrote you four letters and you never—' She stopped. Her jaw worked. 'I'm not angry. It was a long time ago. I just. I always wondered. And you're standing right here, so.'

Joel felt the cold cobalt glass biting into his palm where he'd closed his fist too tight around it.

'Marin,' he said slowly. 'I never got any letters.'

She went very still.

'I wrote you,' he said. 'That's the thing. I wrote you all that fall. Every week, practically, and when you never answered I thought — I figured you'd moved on, that it had just been a summer thing for you, that I'd embarrassed myself. I stopped because it hurt too much. I never got a single letter from you. Not one.'

The tide had turned. Neither of them had noticed, but the thin gray line at the edge of the world had begun, silently, to come in — the water sliding up the channels in the sand, soft and inevitable, reclaiming the ground beneath their feet inch by inch.

'Then where,' Marin said slowly, looking not at him but at something terrible dawning behind her own eyes, 'did all those letters go?'
stories.chapter2_html:
They drove to the cottage because it was the only place with the heat still on, and because neither of them was willing to let the conversation end at the waterline.

His grandmother's house was half-packed, boxes everywhere, the furniture wearing dust sheets like ghosts. Marin stood in the middle of it with her arms wrapped around herself, looking at the walls as though they might tell her something.

'She liked me,' Marin said quietly. 'Your grandmother. That last summer. She used to give me lemonade on the porch and tell me you were too good for this town and you'd leave it the first chance you got.' She turned. 'She collected the mail, Joel. Didn't she. While you were away at school. This was her address. Anything I sent you that fall — it would have come here.'

Joel set down the box he was holding very slowly.

He thought of his grandmother, who had loved him fiercely and narrowly, who had wanted him gone from Halverston and everything in it, who had thought a small-town girl who collected sea glass would only ever hold him back. Who had lived alone in this cottage for twelve more years with a drawer she never let anyone open.

'The rolltop desk,' he said. His voice didn't sound like his own. 'In the back bedroom. She kept it locked. We were going to break into it tomorrow, my brother and I, to clear it out. I have the key on the ring downstairs.'

Marin's eyes were bright and wet and fixed on his. 'Joel,' she said. 'What's in the desk?'

comprehension.question: What does Marin make for a living?
